storm.summary_layperson: The only radar cell in the area is 50 miles to the north and moving away from the cove — no storm threat for the 17:00–22:00 window tonight.
storm.local_effect_blurb: The cell is tracking across interior Connecticut from the north, not sweeping in across Long Island Sound, so the Sound's cooling effect on incoming storms is not a factor tonight.
storm.detail_html:
Regional radar (KOKX, scanned 8:23 PM UTC) shows one cell approximately 50 miles to the north — roughly over the Connecticut River valley — at 40 dBZ and moving toward the ENE. Storm motion was estimated from HRRR steering (radar centroid tracking unavailable this scan) at about 10 knots toward ENE. The closing speed to Greenwich Cove is –4.3 kt: the cell is pulling away, not approaching.

Local instability is absent — CAPE reads 0 J/kg over western Long Island Sound. The NOAA forecast discussion notes scattered late-day shower and thunderstorm potential west of the Hudson River this afternoon, consistent with the cell we're tracking; that activity is moving further away to the northeast.

The storm's approach angle matters: when cells arrive from the southwest or northwest they cross Long Island Sound, and the cool 72°F water can weaken them before arrival. Tonight's cell is tracking overland from the north — it never crosses the Sound — so that protective mechanism is bypassed. But since the cell is moving away, this is moot for tonight.

No NWS watches, advisories, or warnings are in effect. The 17:00–22:00 window looks storm-free. The next organized convective period cited in the forecast discussion is Tuesday–Thursday of next week. Tonight the main story is light wind, not lightning.

wind.regime_label: Sea breeze reinforced by the background SE gradient wind
wind.tactics_blurb: In ESE flow, Greenwich Point shelters the east shore — go left toward Belle Haven and Indian Harbor for pressure. Expect 3–5 kt at RYC vs. 6–7 kt on the open Sound; wind softens after sunset (~8:13 PM) but the SE gradient keeps it from going fully calm.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
This is a sea-breeze day reinforced by the background SE gradient wind. The air–water temperature difference of 6.5°F (inland air 79°F vs. Sound water 72°F) clears the 5°F thermal threshold, and the SE gradient averaging around 7 kt at the airport stations is onshore and adding to the sea breeze rather than fighting it. Eastern Long Island Sound airports show SSE 6–8 kt — a mature sea breeze — while the cove is receiving a more easterly, weaker version due to its position at the western end of the Sound.

The defining tactical challenge tonight is shelter. ESE winds arrive at the cove after passing over the Greenwich Point (Tod's Point) peninsula, which creates a substantial wind shadow along the east shore of the course. RYC already shows 3.6 kt average at 4:30 PM while buoy 44040 reads 7.4 kt over the open water to the south — the cove is roughly half-powered relative to the open Sound. Expect 3–5 kt on the start line throughout the evening, with occasional puffs to 7–8 kt when pressure slots through the gap between Greenwich Point and the CT shore.

Direction runs E–ESE (roughly 88–110°) through the window. HRRR models a subtle backing toward ENE around 18:00–19:00 before returning to ESE after 21:00. The model forecasts 88° E while current western-LIS stations are already at 111° ESE — a 23° gap. The air–water temperature difference (6.5°F) falls below the 8°F gate for a full sea-breeze maturation correction, so this divergence likely reflects HRRR underestimating the ESE orientation at the western end of the Sound. Direction confidence is moderate; expect oscillations of ±10–15° around an ESE mean rather than large persistent shifts.

Sunset is at 8:13 PM (20:13). Under these conditions, the thermal component fades near sunset but the SE gradient remains, so the wind softens gradually rather than collapsing. Expect a step-down from 6–7 kt (Sound-exposed) to around 5 kt after 20:30, with 3–4 kt at RYC in the post-sunset gradient. Last race start around 19:30 is the realistic window for useful breeze; racing after 20:30 will be drifter territory at the cove.

Favored side: left (west, Belle Haven / Indian Harbor). In ESE flow, the east shore near Greenwich Point is in the wind shadow — lighter, shiftier, and unreliable. Go left on the first beat for consistent pressure. Puffs from the ESE gap will track along the west side before spreading across. The main tactical variable tonight is hunting those puffs; there won't be enough wind to simply rail on pressure.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 last reading 78 min ago — over-water wind stale; KBDR and RYC used for current LIS conditions.

HRRR/obs direction divergence: model forecasts E 88°, western-LIS stations observing ESE 111° (+23°) — observations used as baseline for current conditions, HRRR directional trend applied to forecast hours.
